name: Light Travels in a Straight Line — Three Cardboard Experiment
description: Students discover that light travels in a straight line by aligning holes in three cardboard sheets and observing that a candle flame is only visible when all three holes are perfectly aligned.
subject: Physics

pointA.assumption: Students think light can bend around obstacles or scatter in all directions from a source
pointA.blindspot: They don't realize that light always travels in a straight line (rectilinear propagation) and cannot curve around objects

pointB.insight: Light travels in a straight line. When the holes in the cardboards are aligned in a straight line, the flame is visible; when any cardboard is displaced, the flame disappears.
pointB.evidence: Students can predict that moving any one cardboard out of alignment will block the candle flame and explain why using the principle of rectilinear propagation

tutorial.title: Learning Objective
tutorial.content: In this experiment, students will discover that light travels in a straight line (rectilinear propagation). By aligning holes in three cardboard sheets and observing a candle flame, they will see that light can only pass through when all holes are in a straight line.

tutorial.title: Materials & Setup
tutorial.content: You need: 3 cardboard sheets of equal size, 3 stands to hold them upright, 1 candle, 1 matchbox, and a thread. Place the three cardboard sheets together, make a hole at the centre of each, fix them in stands on a table, and use the thread through all three holes to ensure they are perfectly aligned.

tutorial.title: The Procedure
tutorial.content: Step 1: Align the three cardboard sheets using the thread so all holes are in a straight line. Remove the thread. Step 2: Light the candle and place it on one side. Look through the hole from the opposite side — you can see the flame. Step 3: Move one cardboard slightly to the left. Try to see the flame — it disappears. Step 4: Bring it back to position — the flame is visible again. Repeat by displacing the other cardboards one at a time.

tutorial.title: Key Messages
tutorial.content: 1. Light travels in a straight line — this is called rectilinear propagation. 2. When the three holes are aligned, the straight path of light passes through all of them. 3. Displacing any one cardboard breaks the straight-line path and the flame cannot be seen. 4. Real-life examples: headlight beams at night, sunbeams through a dusty window.

tutorial.title: Common Misconceptions
tutorial.content: Students often think light can bend around objects or spread out to fill any space. They may expect to still see the flame even when a cardboard is moved. They need to discover that light only travels in straight lines and cannot curve around obstacles.

procedure: 1. Take three A4-size cardboard sheets of equal size. Place them together and make a hole at the centre of each. 2. Fix the cardboard sheets in stands and place them on a table in a line. 3. Pass a thread through all three holes to verify they are aligned in a straight line. Remove the thread. 4. Light the candle and place it on one side of the cardboard sheets. 5. Look through the hole from the opposite side — you can see the candle flame through all three holes. 6. Move the first cardboard slightly to the left. Try to see the flame from the third cardboard — it disappears. 7. Bring the first cardboard back to its original position — the flame is visible again. 8. Repeat by moving the middle and last cardboards one by one. Each time, the flame disappears when a cardboard is displaced. 9. Ask students: Why can we only see the flame when all three holes are perfectly aligned?

canvasSteps.title: Setup
canvasSteps.description: Three cardboard sheets with holes fixed in stands on a table. A thread passes through all three holes showing they are aligned. Candle and matchbox sit nearby.

canvasSteps.title: Holes Aligned — Flame Visible
canvasSteps.description: Candle is lit on one side. Looking through the hole from the opposite side, the flame is clearly visible through all three aligned holes. A dotted line shows the straight light path.

canvasSteps.title: One Cardboard Displaced — Flame Hidden
canvasSteps.description: The first cardboard is shifted to the left. The flame can no longer be seen. The straight-line path from the candle is blocked by the displaced cardboard.

canvasSteps.title: Restored Alignment — Flame Visible Again
canvasSteps.description: All three cardboards are back in alignment. The flame is visible again, confirming that light travels only in a straight line.

leadingQuestions: Can you see the flame of the candle when all three holes are aligned?
What happens when the first cardboard is moved to the side?
Can you see the flame when any one of the cardboards is displaced?
Why does the flame become visible again when the cardboard is brought back?
What does this tell us about how light travels?
keyMessages: Light travels in a straight line — this property is called rectilinear propagation of light
The candle flame is only visible when all three holes are in a straight line because light cannot bend or curve around obstacles
Displacing any cardboard breaks the straight-line path and blocks the light from reaching your eye
misconceptions: Light can bend around obstacles to reach your eyes
Light spreads out in all directions and can fill any space regardless of obstacles
The flame should still be visible even when a cardboard is moved because light is everywhere

skills.name: Spark
skills.description: Creating curiosity, wonder, and scientific questioning

skills.name: Reach
skills.description: Engaging every student, especially quiet and disengaged
